turns.user_message: Honestly, I don't know why I'm here. I'm not really interested in quitting. I've tried before, and it never works out. I've been smoking for 20 years, and it's just a part of who I am.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the EXPLORATION phase. You know the basics. Now gently explore what situations or emotions make them smoke more — triggers like stress, meals, social settings, boredom. Do NOT suggest any strategies yet. Just understand their situation.

Therapist: Hello, how are you? Welcome to EDosthi. I'm here to help you with tobacco cessation. Would you like to tell me about your tobacco use?
Patient: Honestly, I don't know why I'm here. I'm not really interested in quitting. I've tried before, and it never works out. I've been smoking for 20 years, and it's just a part of who I am.
Therapist: I understand that quitting can be tough, and it's great that you've tried before. Can you tell me more about what's made it difficult for you to quit in the past?

Patient: Well, I've tried nicotine gum, patches, and even cold turkey. But every time, I end up feeling anxious and depressed. It's like my mental health gets worse when I try to quit. And on top of that, I always gain weight when I quit, which is really frustrating.

Therapist: It sounds like you're worried about how quitting might affect your mental health and body image. That makes sense. Have you talked to your doctor about how quitting smoking might impact your depression and anxiety?

Patient: Yeah, I've talked to my doctor. They just tell me to quit smoking, like it's that easy. They don't understand how hard it is. And my family and friends don't get it either. They're all smokers, so they just think I should keep smoking and not worry about it.

Therapist: I understand that quitting can be tough, and it's great that you've tried before. Can you tell me more about what's made it difficult for you to quit in the past?

Patient: Well, I've tried nicotine gum, patches, and even cold turkey. But every time, I end up feeling anxious and depressed. It's like my mental health gets worse when I try to quit. And on top of that, I always gain weight when I quit, which is really frustrating.

Therapist: It sounds like you're worried about how quitting might affect your mental health and body image. That makes sense. Have you talked to your doctor about how quitting smoking might impact your depression and anxiety?

Patient: Yeah, I've talked to my doctor. They just tell me to quit smoking, like it's that easy. They don't understand how hard it is. And my family and friends don't get it either. They're all smokers, so they just think I should keep smoking and not worry about it.

Therapist: I can see why you'd feel like they don't understand. It's tough to quit when you feel like you're going it alone. But I want you to know that I'm here to support you, and we can work together to find ways to manage your anxiety and depression while you quit smoking. Would you be open to exploring some strategies that might help?
Patient: I don't know...I just don't think I can do it. I've failed so many times before.
